title: Exact combinator for τ/σ of a grade-2 wedge
body: When wedge K_i ∧ K_j is articulated, its bit firing is well-defined (AND of parents). But τ(K_i ∧ K_j) and σ(K_i ∧ K_j) as channels — how do they derive from τ(K_i), σ(K_i), τ(K_j), σ(K_j)? Exterior-algebra suggests τ(A ∧ B) = τ_A·σ_B + σ_A·τ_B; σ(A ∧ B) = τ_A·τ_B + σ_A·σ_B (mod 2). Under the 'atoms start τ = σ' decision, all atoms have τ = σ, so this reduces to: τ(wedge) = 0, σ(wedge) = τ_A ∧ τ_B. That collapses to a trivial grade-2 form. Does chirality activate later via articulation patterns, or is there a different combinator?

title: What distributional signal triggers wedge articulation?
body: Under the 'alignment is distribution not gate' principle, we don't articulate wedges when a pair fails to earn Boolean. What IS the signal? Candidates: (1) K-pairs whose four-cell distribution across things has high entropy — potential for further decomposition; (2) K-pairs where oracle-labeled pairs cluster in specific cells and that clustering is under-compressed by the current pool; (3) K-pairs that co-fire beyond what independence would predict (classic mutual information). Need one clean criterion that doesn't smuggle a commit gate back in.

title: Direction of weight gradient if not 'reduce residue to zero'
body: Classical calibration adjusts weights to minimize rank-residue on the oracle. Under 'oracle calibrates not gates', weights tune toward 'configurations where more information accumulates per step'. This needs a concrete scalar to gradient-descend on. Candidates: (1) Shannon entropy of the Belnap cell distribution (higher = more information); (2) oracle-pair expected Belnap-cell population weighted by K weight. Need to pin this before implementing step().

title: How to present the full Belnap distribution as reports
body: Under 'no commit gate', reports enumerate the full Belnap state. This is potentially enormous (things² × K² × 4 cells). Need a principled summarization that preserves the distribution rather than collapsing it. Candidates: (1) per-thing Belnap signature vectors; (2) per-K-pair 4-cell histograms; (3) a 2D UMAP/PCA projection of thing-pair profiles. Pragmatic default: top-N by some information measure, with full-state also available as bulk JSONL.

title: What subset of the corpus is small enough for smoke testing?
body: Full corpus has ~700 things (158 paper + 440 agda + 175 python) and rapidly-growing K-pool as wedges articulate. For smoke testing the closed loop: candidates are (a) just the operationalist definitions across the three sources (3 things, known oracle pair); (b) one Agda file and one Python file with a known correspondence; (c) synthetic minimal corpus. Need to pick one before running step() so we can actually watch the evolution.

title: What do τ and σ observe for an atomic K on a thing?
body: Under p-atoms-are-formal-tau-sigma-channels, the framework does not constrain what τ and σ observe — it is a population choice. Default atom extractor sets τ = σ = 'K fires on X'. Alternative populations (e.g., τ = K appears in odd-depth descendants, σ = K appears in even-depth descendants; or τ = K is directly observed, σ = K is observed via a named relationship) are legitimate future refinements. This question remains open as a RESEARCH direction but does not block implementation — the default population is the maximally free initial state.

title: Demand criterion for step() articulating Rotated K's
body: Tier 3 (Stage 7.1.2) activated the general combinator in _articulate_wedges_batch but did NOT wire step() to auto-articulate Rotated K's.  step()'s pair enumeration uses firing_bitmaps @ firing_bitmaps.T (τ-only co-fire count); Rotated K's with τ=0 under (τκ) rotation of a symmetric atom have all-zero τ columns, so they're invisible to the current pair finder even when present in the pool.  Before step() can propose Rotated articulations, we need (a) an articulation proposer that doesn't go through τ-only co-fire, AND (b) a demand criterion for when to propose which S3-orbit member.  Candidates for (b): (i) oracle-pair σ-orientation residue — pairs with Belnap-earning witness opportunity in σ that no symmetric K captures; (ii) wedge-plateau trigger — at local fixed point of symmetric step(), articulate rotate(K, g) for K's appearing most in uncaptured oracle residue; (iii) CD-doubling analogy from paper Thm 8.6 — each plateau is a doubling step in the Cayley-Dickson tower, adding an S3-orbit member to the pool.  Under the current symmetric-atoms regime, the two orientation-specific oracle scorers (OracleBooleanWitnessTauSigma vs SigmaTau) produce identical counts — so σ-residue can't be detected WITHOUT first having some asymmetric K in the pool.  The trigger may need to be seeded manually on the first iteration (articulate swap + rotate for some K), after which the system can self-reinforce.  Open design question before implementing post-Tier-3 step() activation.

title: Should step()'s co-fire enumeration extend beyond τ-only?
body: step()'s Phase 1 (candidate K-pair enumeration) currently uses firing_bitmaps @ firing_bitmaps.T where firing_bitmaps = state.tau_masks.T.  This finds pairs (K_i, K_j) with n_11 > 0 on the τ channel.  Under asymmetric regime (Tier 3), two K's might co-fire in σ or κ but NOT in τ — step() would miss those pairs.  Three options: (a) extend to union — candidate if co-fire in ANY of τ, σ, κ (one matmul per channel, or single matmul over (tau | sigma) vectors); (b) add configurable channel parameter to step(); (c) decouple pair proposal from channel entirely, using a different primitive like orbit-closure enumeration.  Tied to q-post-tier3-demand-criterion-for-rotated since a demand-driven proposer doesn't need a single fixed channel.  Open.
